segments.s046-g9: از آن خوب‌رخ functions as a causal prepositional phrase ('because of that fair-faced one'). شد is the past of شدن 'to become' and forms the predicate شد دلش پرامید 'his heart became hopeful'. دلش is دل + enclitic possessive ش 'his', so syntactically Subject (دلش) follows the verb (typical of Ferdowsi's word order).
segments.s046-g10: به کین پسر is a prepositional phrase meaning 'a son for revenge' (پسر described as intended for کین). داد is the past 'gave' and takes دل را as the (definite-marked) indirect/affected object: داد دل را نوید = 'gave the heart a promise/tidings'. نوید here means 'promise/omen' (the thing given).
segments.s046-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. هنگامه زادن is a genitive construction 'the moment of birth' (هنگامه qualifying زادن). آمد پدید is a poetic double-verb sequence meaning 'came into being/appeared' (آمد + پدید emphasizing emergence).
segments.s046-g12: یکی دختر آمد = 'a certain daughter came' (i.e. was born). ز ماه آفرید is a compact poetic clause 'was made/created from the moon' (ز = 'from', آفرید past 'created'); word order places the origin phrase after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s046-g13: جهانی here functions as a collective subject 'the world / all people'. گرفتند is the third-person plural past 'they took' and is followed by the verbal noun with enclitic پروردنش (پروردن + ش 'her') meaning 'their rearing of her' — together 'the world took to rearing her'.
segments.s046-g14: برآمد is past 'arose/grew up'. به ناز و بزرگی تنش is a prepositional phrase describing the manner/qualities with which she grew: به ناز 'in (delicate) pampering/affection' and به بزرگی تنش 'in the greatness/size of her body'. ش is the possessive enclitic 'her'.
segments.s046-g15: مر is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'to me' or 'tell me' (dative/emphatic). آن ماه‌رخ را is the definite object 'that moon-faced (woman)', marked by را. ز سر تا به پای is an idiomatic range 'from head to foot' (ز ... تا ... = 'from ... to ...').
segments.s046-g16: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. گفتی here is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (note: the provided sense list did not include this verbal sense, so it is reported with -1 below). مگر introduces a corrective or tentative question 'perhaps / did you not (say)'. ایرجستی is an epithet/adjectival form relating the woman to Iraj (i.e. 'Iraj-like' or 'of Iraj'), and به جای = 'in place / in stead'. Together the clause addresses the addressee's reported remark: 'you said, perhaps, she was Iraj in stead?'
segments.s046-g57: چه begins an exclamatory listing (“what…!”). اسپان (pl. of اسپ) is the noun ‘horses/steeds’; تازی is an adjectival modifier ‘Arabian’ placed after the noun (typical Persian poetic order). به + زرین + ستام is a prepositional phrase where به introduces the accompaniment/place (‘in/with’), زرین is the adjective ‘golden’ preceding the head noun ستام; the whole phrase therefore means ‘with/in golden ستام’ (an archaic/noun for a horse‑fitting or caparison here).
segments.s046-g58: Repeating the exclamative چه. شمشیر is the head noun ‘sword’; هندی is the adjectival qualifier ‘Indian’ placed after the noun (poetic order). به زرین نیام is the same prepositional+adjective+noun pattern as above, meaning ‘in/with a golden scabbard’ (نیام = scabbard). The line pairs two parallel noun phrases for rhetorical effect.
segments.s046-g59: چه again introduces an exclamation. از here lists sources/items ‘from/of’ (introducing the following series). جوشن and زره are synonyms meaning ‘armor/curass’; ترگ ‘rags/shreds’ is inserted as a contrast; رومی qualifies زره as ‘Roman/Byzantine’. The sequence is a poetic piled list of types of armor/garments.
segments.s046-g60: گشادند is a past‑plural verb (گشادن → گشادند) meaning ‘they opened/loosened/widened’. مر is the enclitic/old form ‘to me’ (dat. ‘me’). بندها را گره is the object: بندها ‘bonds/fetters’ with the object marker را, followed by گره ‘knot’ (i.e. ‘the knots of the bonds’). Word order is inverted for emphasis: verb precedes the indirect object and then the direct object.
segments.s046-g61: کمانهای is the plural ‘bows’. چاچی follows as an attributive name or adjective (here likely a type/style name; the ordinary lexical senses given do not fit the poetic proper name usage). وتیر should be read as و + تیر (‘and arrow’) joined in the text; خدنگ either modifies تیر (‘barbed/poisoned arrow’) or stands as a separate noun ‘dagger/short sword’. The phrase lists weapons in rapid apposition.
segments.s046-g62: سپرهای is ‘shields’ (plural) with the postpositive adjective چینی ‘Chinese’. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. ژوپین is a padded jacket/gambeson (a protective garment) and جنگ (‘war’) qualifies it (ژوپین جنگ = ‘gambesons for war’ or ‘warlike gambesons’). The nouns and adjectives are in the usual Persian order (head then modifiers) in this line.
segments.s046-g63: برین گونه is a frozen poetic expression meaning ‘in this manner/so’ (بر + این/ین + گونه = ‘upon/this way’). آراسته is the perfect/passive participle ‘adorned/arrayed’ modifying گنجها ‘treasures’. Word order is noun phrase with participial adjective following the manner phrase.
segments.s046-g64: که introduces a relative clause ‘which/that’. بودش is the past copula with the enclitic referring back to گنجها (‘there was/there were for it’). به گرد آمده is a compound meaning ‘gathered around’ (به + گرد ‘around’ + آمده past participle ‘come/arrived’). رنجها is the subject of that subordinate clause meaning ‘the hardships/labors’ — literally, ‘the hardships had come together around it.’
segments.s046-g25: This is a short reporting clause: 'بدو' (to him) precedes the verb 'گفت' (said) in the poetic word order. 'موبد' names the speaker (the mobad/priest). 'که‌ای' is a vocative interjection introducing direct speech ('O ...'), and 'تاجور' is the addressee’s title. Note that some items (like 'گفت' and the vocative 'که‌ای') are idiomatic/poetic usages not fully captured by the modern sense-list here.
segments.s046-g26: 'یکی' here functions as an indefinite/accusative ('someone' / 'one [person]'). 'شَادکن' is an imperative verb 'make happy/cheer' addressing the addressee. 'دل' is the direct object ('heart'). The prepositional phrase 'به ایرج' ('toward Iraj') follows, and 'نگر' is a second imperative 'look!' (imperative placed at the end, as is common in poetic command sequences).
segments.s046-g27: 'جهان‌بخش' (literally 'world‑bestower') is the possessor/topic; the particle 'را' here marks that noun for emphasis/definite reference in this poetic construction. The predicate is 'لب پر از خنده شد' — 'لب' (lip), 'پر از' (full of), 'خنده' (laughter/smile), and 'شد' is the past of شدن 'became'. Word order places the possessor with 'را' before the predicate, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s046-g28: 'تو گفتی' is simple past 'you said'. 'مگر' introduces a surprised or rhetorical question here ('could it be / is it possible that'). 'ایرجش' is the name 'Iraj' with the enclitic '-ش' (3rd-person clitic) attached in the poem; syntactically it functions as the subject of the following predicate. 'زنده شد' = 'became alive' (past), i.e., 'has come back to life.'
segments.s046-g29: 'نهاد' here is the past tense of نهادن 'placed, put'. 'آن گرانمایه را' is the definite object ('that precious one') marked by 'را'. 'برکنار' is an adverb/phrase meaning 'aside' (i.e., laid aside). The verb precedes or follows adverbial complements flexibly in epic verse; here the whole expression means 'laid that noble one aside.'
segments.s046-g30: 'نیایش' is 'prayer; supplication'. 'همی' gives a durative/habitual sense ('kept on / continued to'). 'کرد' is the past tense auxiliary/lexical verb 'did' (here 'performed [prayer]'). 'با کردگار' is a prepositional phrase 'with / to the Creator' — in older usage 'با' can mark the addressee of prayer, so the sense is 'prayed to the Creator.'
segments.s046-g31: 'همی گفت' is the habitual/past continuous ('kept saying'). The clause 'کاین روز فرخنده باد' is an optative/wish clause introduced by 'کاین' ('this') and closed by the particle 'باد' meaning 'may it be' — 'May this day be auspicious.'
segments.s046-g32: The second wish is elliptical: 'دل بدسگالان ما کنده باد' — 'دل' (hearts) is the theme, 'بدسگالان ما' means 'our ill‑omened ones' (i.e., ill‑wishers or those bringing bad augury to us), 'کنده' (uprooted/torn away) is a past participle used in the optative construction, and final 'باد' again gives the optative 'may'. So literally: 'May the hearts of our ill‑omened ones be uprooted.'
segments.s046-g73: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase marking what is being acclaimed (‘to/for kingship’). برو here is an archaic/poetic contraction of بر او (‘upon him’), linking the acclaim to the person (not the literal imperative “go”); خواندند is past tense, 3rd-person plural, meaning “they cried/uttered.” Together آفرین خواندند = “they cried ‘afarin’ (bravo).”
segments.s046-g74: زبرجد is the direct object (gems/peridots) placed before the verb for poetic ordering; به تاجش is a locative/directional phrase ‘onto his crown’ (تاج + possessive -ش = his crown). برافشاندند is past, 3rd-person plural, ‘they scattered/showered (upon).’
segments.s046-g75: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ جشنی بد is a noun + adjective ‘an evil festival,’ and این روزگار بزرگ (“this great age/time”) follows to qualify which festival it is; poetic word order places modifiers after or around the head noun.
segments.s046-g76: شده is the past participle/auxiliary signalling ‘had been made / was held.’ در جهان is locative ‘in the world.’ میش پیدا is an inverted phrase meaning ‘a sheep appeared’ (پیدا functioning as ‘appeared/visible’), and ز گرگ uses the poetic preposition ز ‘from/of’ to indicate arising/appearance in relation to wolves — i.e., ‘among/from wolves’ (figurative).
segments.s046-g77: سپهدار is a title/noun ‘the commander.’ چون introduces a simile ‘like,’ and the following name/epithet قارن کاوگان functions as the point of comparison. In poetic lists like this, short noun phrases stand apposed without an explicit verb.
segments.s046-g78: سپهکش (‘his/one of his champions’ or ‘army-slayer’) names another figure being compared; چو شیروی gives a simile ‘like Shiruy’ (either a proper name or ‘lion-like’), و چون آوگان adds another comparison ‘and like the chanters/voices’—the conjunction و links the two similes.
segments.s046-g79: چو … شد ساخته is a temporal clause: چو = ‘when,’ شد + ساخته = ‘had been made/arranged’ (past auxiliary + participle). کار لشکر همه is the noun phrase ‘the whole/work of the army’ (کار = affair/work; لشکر = army; همه = all/entire), with poetic adjective/quantifier ordering.
segments.s046-g80: برآمد is past ‘arose/emerged/issued forth.’ سر شهریار is a nominal phrase: سر here is used metonymically as ‘the head/chief/leader’ (i.e., the sovereign himself) and شهریار = king. از رمه is a source phrase ‘from the throng’ (از = from; رمه used figuratively as a crowd/multitude).
segments.s046-g49: هنرها 'the arts/skills' is the head noun (plural). که آید is a relative/temporal clause ('that/which come' or 'that are of use'); آید is present-tense classical/persian literary verb 'comes' and links back to هنرها. شهان را is the definite plural object marked by را ('to/for the kings'), and به کار is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ('to/for use') where به governs کار; together the clause says 'the arts that come to use for kings.'
segments.s046-g50: بیاموختش is an archaic/perfective verb form built on آموختن with the attached object pronoun -ش ('it/them') — here 'taught them'; word order has the verb before the postposed agent, a common poetic ordering. نامور شهریار is the agent/nominative phrase 'the renowned sovereign' (نامور 'renowned' modifying شهریار 'sovereign/king').
segments.s046-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. چشم و دل is a coordinated noun phrase 'eye(s) and heart' (here metonymically 'sight and heart' or 'attention and feeling'). پادشا (shortened poetic form of پادشاه) is the possessor/agent 'the king'. باز شد is a past tense compound ('opened' / 'became open'), where شد is the past auxiliary meaning 'became/was.'
segments.s046-g52: سپه is the subject 'the army/host'. نیز 'also' marks addition. با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him', and هم‌آواز شد is a predicate meaning 'became of one accord' or 'fell into harmony' (هم‌آواز = 'in unison', شد = past auxiliary 'became'). Word order is Subject–(adverb)–prepositional phrase–predicate, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s046-g53: This is a nominal list of gifts where the (implied or stated) giver/agent precedes or follows the objects in poetic order. نیا appears before the list and here functions as a nominative/agentive element (the supplied senses suggest 'ancestor' or similar; contextually it functions as the doer). تخت زرین and گرز گران are two coordinated noun phrases: تخت 'throne' modified postpositively by the adjective زرین 'golden', and گرز 'mace/club' modified by گران 'heavy/precious.'
segments.s046-g54: بدو داد is the verbal core 'gave to him' where بدو is the fused prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او compressed) and داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. The conjunction و links the next object phrase پیروزه تاج سران, where پیروزه functions as an adjective (here likely 'turquoise' as a material/colour) modifying تاج 'crown', and سران is a genitive/possessive plural 'of the chiefs/leaders.'
segments.s046-g55: This is a stacked noun phrase describing luxurious tent‑furnishings: سراپرده 'inner hanging/curtain' followed by دیبه 'fine silk/brocade' and the compound adjective هفت‌رنگ 'seven‑colored' modifying دیبه. Adjectives and descriptors follow their nouns in this poetic listing.
segments.s046-g56: بدو is again the fused prepositional phrase 'to him' (به + او). اندرون 'inside/within' specifies location, and خیمه‌های پلنگ is the possessed/attributive noun 'tents of (or patterned like) leopard' (خیمه‌های = 'tents' with plural marker, پلنگ = 'leopard' used as an epithet/descriptor). Together the segment says 'and within (unto) him, leopard‑tents.'
segments.s046-g1: برآمد is a past verb 'he rose/rose up'. برین here is a poetic spatial phrase made of بر ('on/upon') + ین (an older/orthographic form of 'that' or 'این'), so together 'to that high/above place'. نیز 'also' adds that this action is in addition to something before, and یک چندگاه is a temporal adverbial 'for a while / for a short time' modifying the following verb phrase.
segments.s046-g2: شبستان ایرج is a genitival phrase 'Irāj's bedchamber/inner chamber' (possessive relation implied without را), followed by the compound verb نگه کرد where نگه 'watch/gaze' + کرد (do/make) yields 'kept watch'. The subject شاه comes after the verb (inverted/poetic word order): 'the king kept watch'.
segments.s046-g3: یکی 'a certain' (indefinite), followed by adjectival/participial descriptors خوب 'beautiful' and چهره پرستنده, a compound in which چهره 'face' and پرستنده (literally 'admirer/devotee') function to indicate that her face is admired or 'face‑adored'. دید is the past verb 'saw' and closes the clause (standard Subject–Modifiers–Verb order).
segments.s046-g4: کجا here functions as an archaic/poetic relative 'whose' (literally 'where'), so 'کجا نام او بود' = 'whose name was ...'. نام او بود is a simple past copular clause 'her name was', and ماه‌آفرید is the proper name in predication position.
segments.s046-g5: که introduces a causal/subordinate clause 'for/that'. ایرج is the subject. برو is a contracted/poetic form functioning as 'بر او' = 'toward/for her' (here marking the object of affection). مهر بسیار داشت is a past-tense predicate 'had great love/affection' (داشت as simple past of داشتن).
segments.s046-g6: This clause supplies the fated result: قضا 'fate' is the topical subject (marked by را), کنیزک ازو 'a little maid from her' is the thing associated with that fate, and بار داشت is an idiomatic phrasing with داشت ('had') where بار ('burden' / metaphorical 'gift/occasion') expresses that fate 'laid upon / gave' this small maid — i.e., fate provided him a little slave/maid.
segments.s046-g7: پری چهره is a compound 'fairy‑faced' (literally 'fairy face' used adjectivally) topicalized by را, so 'the fair‑faced one' is put forward; بچه بود is a past clause 'had a child / there was a child' and در نهان ('in secret') is an adverbial phrase modifying the event, placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s046-g8: از آن is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of that / from that'. شاد شد is a two-word past predicate 'became glad' (شد as past of شدن). شهریار جهان is the subject phrase 'the king (lord) of the world' placed after the verb in inverted/poetic order.
segments.s046-g17: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). بر جست is a compound verbal idea meaning 'sprang up' (بر is a verbal prefix here attached to جست 'leap'). آمدش is 'came to her/it' (simple past sense) and هنگام شو(ی) ('هنگام' + the verb 'شوی') means 'the time/season of becoming' or 'the hour arrived' — شوی is the verb شدن in an archaic/poetic form. Together the clause means 'when she sprang up and her time arrived.'
segments.s046-g18: چو here again sets a comparison/temporal 'when/as'; پروین is used as a simile (the Pleiades) and شدش is the past of شدن with the enclitic -ش referring to 'her' ('became for her' / 'her appearance became'). روی is 'face.' The second half uses چون 'like/as' + مشک 'musk' + موی 'hair' to give a parallel simile: 'her face like the Pleiades and her hair like musk.'
segments.s046-g19: نیا is used as a proper name/title (here treated as the ancestor/name). نامزد کرد is a light verb construction: نامزد 'betrothed/suitor' used with کرد 'made' = 'betrothed (someone).' شویش is an opaque item here (the form suggests a pronoun/possessive or an archaic/neologistic word) and does not match the supplied noun senses, so it is treated as non-matching in the provided sense list; پشنگ is a personal name.
segments.s046-g20: بدو is the allative/enclitic 'to him/to her/to it' (not the imperative 'run!'). داد is the past of دادن 'gave.' و is the coordinating 'and.' چندی here means 'for a while.' برآمد is past 'arose/issued forth' and درنگ is a noun meaning 'delay/pause' — the clause says that after the giving there followed a short pause.
segments.s046-g21: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' / 'one (a)'; پور is 'son' or 'descendant.' زاد can be read as the verbal/noun root for birth/origin ('was born' / 'birth' depending on syntax); آن points back to 'that'; هنرمند 'skillful' modifies ماه, where ماه is a poetic epithet for a beautiful person (literally 'moon' but here 'beautiful one'). The phrase means 'a son was born to that skillful/beautiful one.'
segments.s046-g22: چگونه is an exclamatory 'how' (introducing admiration). سزاوار is an adjective 'fit/deserving.' تخت is 'throne' and کلاه here functions as 'crown' (idiomatic pairing تخت و کلاه = throne and crown). The segment asks rhetorically how well suited the newborn son was to rulership.
segments.s046-g23: چو 'when'; از indicates separation 'from'; مادر مهربان is 'the kind/affectionate mother.' شد جدا is a periphrastic past: شد ('became/happened') + جدا ('apart') = 'was separated' or 'became apart.' The clause means 'when he was separated from his kind mother.'
segments.s046-g24: سبک here functions adverbially ('light(ly)' / briskly). تاختندش is the past plural of تاختن with the object enclitic -ش: 'they galloped/rode off him/with him' or more generally 'they hurried him by riding.' به نزد is the allative 'to the presence of / to (someone).' نیا is the same name/title as above. The clause means they quickly took or hastened him to Niyâ.
segments.s046-g33: 'همان کز ... کرد یاد' is a relative clause: 'همان' = 'that/same one' introduces the head, while 'کز' here is the relative/prepositional connector 'who/that' (introducing the clause). 'جهان‌آفرین' is the agent 'Creator of the World' and 'کرد یاد' is a light‑verb construction where 'کرد' ('made/caused') combines with 'یاد' ('remembrance') to mean 'remembered' or 'caused to be remembered' (literal 'made remembrance'). Word order is topic (همان) + relative clause with verb at the end, as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s046-g34: Two coordinated past verbs: 'ببخشود' (3rd sg. past) = 'he forgave/ granted' is followed by 'و' = 'and'. The clause 'دیده بدو باز داد' has the object 'دیده' ('eye') and indirect object 'بدو' ('to him') before the verb 'داد' (past 'gave'), with the adverb 'باز' ('again') modifying how the eye was given. This follows Persian tendency to place objects before the verb; both verbs share the same subject implied from context.
segments.s046-g35: Temporal clause introduced by 'چو' = 'when'. 'فریدون' is the subject, 'روشن جهان را' is the object phrase with 'روشن' ('bright') qualifying 'جهان' ('world') and the object marker 'را' marking definiteness. 'بدید' is 3rd person past of 'دیدن' = 'saw'.
segments.s046-g36: 'به' is the preposition 'at/to' heading the prepositional object 'به چهر نوآمد' ('at/to the newly arrived face'); 'نوآمد' ('newly come') qualifies 'چهر' ('face/appearance'). 'سبک' here functions as an adverbial modifier meaning 'light/gentle' and modifies the verb 'بنگرید' (past 3rd sg. of نگریستن = 'looked'). Word order places the prepositional phrase and modifiers before the verb.
segments.s046-g37: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'); 'کز' here has the source sense 'from' introducing the content of what he said ('from pure mother and father ...'). 'پاک مام و پدر' is a coordinated noun phrase 'pure mother and father' serving as the source in the subordinate clause. Note: 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (the provided sense-list did not include the verb sense, so index -1 is used).
segments.s046-g38: 'یکی' = 'one / a single' (indefinite numeral); 'شاخ' ('branch') is used metaphorically for a scion/offspring and is modified by the adjective 'شایسته' ('worthy'). 'آمد' is the past verb 'came' and the phrase 'آمد به بر' (literally 'came to/upon (the) bosom/side') is an idiomatic way in classical Persian to say 'was born' or 'appeared'.
segments.s046-g39: 'می‌روشن' is literally a present plural 'they shine' but here functions attributively/poetically as 'shining' (a shining one), followed by the past verb 'آمد' = 'came'—a common poetic inversion where the participial/attributive form precedes the verb. 'ز' = 'from' introduces the source phrase 'پرمایه جام' = 'cup full of substance' (i.e., a rich/filled cup).
segments.s046-g40: 'مر' is an archaic/object form meaning 'to me' (dat. 'for me'). 'آن چهر' = 'that face' is the topic; 'دارد' is present (or gnomic) 'has' or 'bears' and combines with following nominal 'منوچهر نام' = 'the name Manuchehr' — i.e., 'that face, to me, has the name Manuchehr' = 'I call that face Manuchehr.'
segments.s046-g41: چنان is an adverb meaning 'so/thus' modifying the following verb. پروریدش is the past tense 'he reared / raised him' where the enclitic -ش is the 3rd-person object 'him'. که introduces the result clause. باد هوا is a poetic noun phrase literally 'wind (باد) of هوا'; here هوا is used in the sense of 'desire/whim' and combines with باد to mean 'a gust of whim/desire'.
segments.s046-g42: This is the result clause begun by که. برو is the verb 'to go/blow' (here an intransitive sense 'to pass, to blow'), بر is the directional particle 'over / upon', گذشتی is the past participial/past form 'passing / passing by', and نبودی روا is a compact copular/epistolary construction: نبودی (negative past of بودن) + روا ('proper/permissible') = 'it would not have been proper/permissible'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect, with the predicate (نبودی روا) at the end.
segments.s046-g43: پرستنده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a worshipper / admirer' (the -ای marks indefiniteness). کش is the root 'to pull / draw' used here in a verbal sense linked to the following phrase, and به بر داشتی is a verbal phrase: به بر 'to the arm/into the embrace' + داشتی (past 2nd-person) 'you would have had / you would have taken'—the whole expresses a counterfactual/result: 'you would have drawn/taken a worshipper to your bosom.'
segments.s046-g44: زمین را uses را as the definite/direct-object/emphatic marker 'the earth'. به پی means 'in pursuit of / after', هیچ = 'nothing' (negating the pursuit), and نگذاشتی is the past negative of گذاشتن 'you did not let/you would not have left/abandoned'. The clause says you would not have abandoned the earth in pursuit of nothing.
segments.s046-g45: به پای اندرش = 'at his feet': به پای ('to/at the foot') + اندر + -ش (3rd-person possessive 'his'). مشک سارا is a noun phrase 'musk (مشک) Sāra' — here سارا appears as the specific epithet/name attached to the musk; بدی is the archaic past form 'you gave/placed' (note: the supplied sense list for بدی in the data is for the adjective 'bad'/'evil', so the verbal sense is not listed).
segments.s046-g46: روان here is used adjectivally 'flowing / streaming' qualifying چتر دیبا 'a brocade canopy'. بر سرش = 'on/over his head' (بر = on/upon, سر + -ش = his head). بدی (again) is the archaic past 'you gave/you set'. The adjective precedes the noun (common in poetic diction) and the verb (بدی) closes the clause.
segments.s046-g47: چنین 'thus/so' sets the manner. تا is the temporal conjunction 'until'. برآمد is past 'arose/occurred' and برو سالیان is a poetic clause 'years went by' (برو as intransitive 'go' applied to سالیان 'years'); together they indicate the passing of time ('until years had passed').
segments.s046-g48: نیامدش is the negative past 'did not come to him' with enclitic -ش 'to him'. ز اختر = 'from the star(s)' (ز = from, اختر = star), زمانی here means 'for a while / for some time', and زیان is the noun 'harm / loss'. The clause states that for a time no harm came to him from the stars.
segments.s046-g65: This is a compact nominal+verb clause: سراسر ('the entire/whole') modifies سزای منوچهر (سزا = 'recompense/desert' with منوچهر as possessor), and the past verb دید ('he saw') closes the clause. Sزَآ here is archaic/poetic for 'that which one deserves' (reward or recompense).
segments.s046-g66: دل خویش را is the definite object (دل خویش = 'his own heart', را marks it). زو is the poetic contraction of از او ('from/for him'), followed by the predicate phrase پر از مهر ('full of love'); final دید is the same past verb 'he saw', linking this object clause to the verb. In short: 'he saw his heart, with respect to him, full of love.'
segments.s046-g67: A noun phrase in which کلید ('key') is located by the prepositional phrase در گنج آراسته: در ('in') + گنج ('treasure/treasury') modified by آراسته ('adorned'). The adjective آراسته follows the noun in poetic word order.
segments.s046-g68: به introduces the indirect object ('to/for'), گنجور او reads as 'his گنجور' (here functioning as treasury or the treasurer connected to the treasury), and داد is the past verb 'gave'. با خواسته is an adverbial phrase 'with (his) wish' i.e. 'as requested' or 'at his request'. Note the possessive ordering גنجور او instead of a suffixal form is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s046-g69: A left-branching noun phrase: همه ('all') quantifies پهلوانان ('champions/heroes'), modified by the genitive لشکرش ('his army'); را is the object marker, marking this whole NP as an object of an implied or following verb. The order (quantifier + noun + possessor + را) is idiomatic in epic Persian.
segments.s046-g70: Parallel to the previous segment: همه ('all') + نامداران ('renowned men') + کشورش ('his country') with را again marking the NP as object. The repetition is a poetic accumulation of those summoned.
segments.s046-g71: بفرمود is an honorific/past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا here functions as a purposive/result complementizer 'so that' or 'and then', introducing the subordinate clause پیش او آمدند ('they came before him/in his presence') with آمدند in past plural. The clause structure is 'He commanded, and so they came before him.'
segments.s046-g72: همه is the subject 'all/everyone', با دلی کینه‌جو is an adverbial phrase 'with a vengeful heart' (با + دلی, where کینه‌جو modifies دلی), and آمدند is the plural past 'they came'. Together: 'all came with vengeful hearts.'

sentences.s046-s5: Because of that fair-faced one his heart became hopeful; he gave the heart the promise of a son for vengeance.
sentences.s046-s6: When the moment of birth arrived, one daughter appeared, created from the moon.
sentences.s046-s7: The whole world took her up and reared her; she grew up in delicacy and the greatness of her body.
sentences.s046-s8: Tell me of that moon-faced one from head to foot — you said, perhaps, she was (like) Iraj in place?
sentences.s046-s29: What Arabian steeds in golden saddles, what Indian swords in golden scabbards.
sentences.s046-s30: What cuirasses, rags, and Roman armor—they loosened the knots of my bonds.
sentences.s046-s31: Bows of Chachi and arrows, daggers; Chinese shields and padded gambesons of war.
sentences.s046-s32: Treasures so arrayed — round which hardships had gathered.
sentences.s046-s13: The mobad said to him, “O Tajur, cheer someone’s heart; look toward Iraj.”
sentences.s046-s14: The world‑bestower’s lip became full of laughter; you said, “Could it be that Iraj is alive?”
sentences.s046-s15: He placed that precious one aside and continued to pray to the Creator.
sentences.s046-s16: He kept saying, 'May this day be auspicious; may the hearts of our ill‑omened (ill‑wishers) be torn away.'
sentences.s046-s37: They cried “Bravo to his kingship” and showered his crown with peridots.
sentences.s046-s38: When this great, evil festival of the age was held, a sheep appeared in the world among wolves.
sentences.s046-s39: The commander, like Qaran Kaavgan; his champion, like Shiruy, and like the chanters.
sentences.s046-s40: When all the army’s affairs had been arranged, the sovereign emerged from the throng.
sentences.s046-s25: The famed sovereign taught the arts that are of use to kings.
sentences.s046-s26: When the king's eyes and heart opened, the army likewise fell in with him in accord.
sentences.s046-s27: He gave (to him) the golden throne and the heavy mace, and the turquoise crown of the chiefs.
sentences.s046-s28: Inner hangings of brocade, seven‑colored, and within to him the leopard‑tents.
sentences.s046-s1: The king rose up there as well and for a short while kept watch over Irāj's bedchamber.
sentences.s046-s2: He saw a certain beautiful one, admired for her face; her name was Māh-āfrīd.
sentences.s046-s3: For Irāj had great affection for her; by fate he had a little maid from her.
sentences.s046-s4: The fairy‑faced woman had a child in secret; because of that the king of the world was glad.
sentences.s046-s9: When she leapt up and the hour of her coming (to be) arrived, her face shone like the Pleiades and her hair like musk.
sentences.s046-s10: Niyâ made (someone) betrothed; he gave Pashang to him/her, and for a while there was a pause.
sentences.s046-s11: A son was born to that skillful (moon-like) beauty — how fitting he was for throne and crown!
sentences.s046-s12: When he was parted from his kind mother, they hastened him lightly to Niyâ's presence.
sentences.s046-s17: That very one whom the Creator of the World remembered, He pardoned and restored his sight.
sentences.s046-s18: When Fereydun saw the bright world, he looked at the newly arrived face with a light (gentle) gaze.
sentences.s046-s19: Thus he said that from pure mother and father a single worthy scion came forth.
sentences.s046-s20: From the cup of rich substance a shining one came; that face, to me, bears the name Manuchehr.
sentences.s046-s21: He reared him so that, had a gust of whim blown toward him, it would not have been proper for it to pass him by.
sentences.s046-s22: You would have taken a worshipper into your arms; you would not have left the earth in pursuit of nothing.
sentences.s046-s23: At his feet you placed fragrant musk; over his head you set a flowing brocade canopy.
sentences.s046-s24: Thus it went on until years passed; no harm came to him from the stars for a time.
sentences.s046-s33: He beheld the whole recompense of Manuchehr; he saw his own heart filled with love for him.
sentences.s046-s34: He gave the key, kept in the adorned treasure-chamber, to his treasury/treasurer, at the requested wish.
sentences.s046-s35: All the champions of his army, all the renowned men of his country.
sentences.s046-s36: He ordered, so that they came before him; all of them came with vengeful hearts.
